region_of_origin: Tamil Nadu, India; also traditional in Singapore Indian community

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for Athirasam. FOODON_03301116 (deep-fried dough food product) is the closest defensible parent class. Athirasam is a deep-fried jaggery-sweetened rice-flour dough product of South Indian origin.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (deep fried dough-based snack) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. No exact Athirasam entry exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Athirasam or a sufficiently close analogue in the clinical food hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Athirasam is one piece weighing approximately 40 g, providing around 173 kcal, 26 g carbohydrates, and 14.7 g sugar.

nutrient_source.title: Athirasam — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Athirasam (cr_id F11100001). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and is estimated from analogous deep-fried jaggery dough products in USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy, fried dough, sweet) as approximately 2.1 g/100g.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of deep-fried sweet dough products — analogous estimate based on fried dough / sweet fried pastry entries
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Athirasam exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 76 (high) based on the closest analogous entry: deep-fried sweet dough / fried dough pastry with refined flour and sugar, which reports GI values in the range 74–78 in the University of Sydney GI Database. Jaggery has a GI of approximately 84 (peer-reviewed: Bhatt et al., 2021, Journal of Food Science and Technology), but the combined matrix of rice flour, wheat flour, and deep-frying moderates the overall GI to approximately 76. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (76 × 26.28) / 100 = 19.97, rounded to 19.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sugar content (36.7 g/100g) with low dietary fibre (1.5 g/100g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and promotes weight gain, independently elevating blood pressure risk.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Athirasam due to its very high sugar load (36.7 g/100g) and low fibre (1.5 g/100g), which promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and weight gain per AHA 2018 dietary guidelines; if consumed, restrict to no more than one small piece (≤40 g) on an occasional basis and avoid pairing with other high-sugar foods or beverages.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar is 36.7 g/100g (exceeding the 20 g/100g glycaemic ceiling) and total carbohydrates are 65.7 g/100g with only 1.5 g/100g dietary fibre, producing a rapid and severe postprandial blood glucose spike; estimated GI of 76 (high) confirms significant glycaemic impact.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid Athirasam or limit intake to a very small occasional portion (≤20 g) with close blood glucose monitoring, as its high GI (estimated 76), very high sugar content (36.7 g/100g), and low fibre (1.5 g/100g) cause a rapid and severe postprandial glucose excursion; it must not be consumed on an empty stomach or alongside other high-carbohydrate foods.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and increases total fat to 17.3 g/100g; combined with 36.7 g/100g sugar, this food pattern is associated with elevated triglycerides, LDL oxidation, and endothelial dysfunction.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid or very strictly limit Athirasam because its deep-fried preparation (17.3 g fat/100g, estimated 2.1 g saturated fat/100g) combined with very high sugar (36.7 g/100g) promotes elevated triglycerides, LDL oxidation, and systemic inflammation, all of which are independent risk factors for adverse cardiovascular events per ACC/AHA 2019 dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory AGEs and the 36.7 g/100g refined sugar content promotes inflammatory cytokine production, worsening joint inflammation in RA patients.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Athirasam as its deep-fried preparation generates AGEs that exacerbate joint inflammation, and its very high sugar content (36.7 g/100g) promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine production; if consumed at all, limit to a very small portion and pair with anti-inflammatory foods such as turmeric-based beverages.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Athirasam (Tamil: அதிரசம்) is a traditional South Indian deep-fried sweet pastry originating from Tamil Nadu, India, and widely prepared within the Tamil community in Singapore. It is made from a fermented dough of rice flour (sometimes blended with a small proportion of wheat flour), jaggery (unrefined cane sugar), and cardamom powder, which is shaped into flat discs and deep-fried in oil until golden brown. The result is a dense, chewy, slightly crispy exterior pastry with a rich caramel-like sweetness from the jaggery and a warm aromatic note from the cardamom. Athirasam is traditionally prepared during festivals such as Diwali and Karthigai Deepam, and is a culturally significant celebratory sweet in Tamil households. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 433 kcal, 3.6 g protein, 17.3 g fat, 65.7 g carbohydrates, 36.7 g sugar, 1.5 g dietary fibre, and 63 mg sodium. Its very high sugar content (36.7 g/100g) and high estimated glycemic index (approximately 76) make it a high-concern food for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. The deep-frying process also generates pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), making it unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. A typical serving is one piece (approximately 40 g), providing around 173 kcal and 14.7 g sugar. Despite its high energy density and sugar load, Athirasam provides modest amounts of calcium (68 mg/100g) and iron (0.7 mg/100g) from the jaggery and rice flour base.

What is the glycemic index of Athirasam?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11100001) with a confidence of 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from a closely analogous USDA SR Legacy entry (deep-fried sweet dough) and documented accordingly. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on deep-fried sweet dough pastry entries, with supporting peer-reviewed jaggery GI data. Multilingual names are verified against standard Tamil orthography and common transliterations. All ontology codes are defensible approximations with gap notes provided.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.1 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried sweet dough (closest approved-source analogue); HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for Athirasam are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. 3. GI of 76 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Athirasam exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. 4. FoodOn URI FOODON_03301116 and FoodEx2 code A0FKP are approximate matches; no exact ontology entry exists for Athirasam. 5. Chinese names (阿提拉萨姆 / 阿提拉薩姆) and Hindi name (अथिरसम) are phonetic transliterations as no established Chinese or Hindi culinary name exists for this Tamil-specific sweet. 6. All core macros (protein_g 3.6, fat_g 17.3, carbohydrate_g 65.7) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification is required.
